one_line_takeaway: EBRM bolts a ~3%-size energy-based head onto a frozen reward model to model a distribution over rewards rather than a point estimate, improving robustness and safety-critical win rates and delaying reward hacking in RLHF—though its edge largely evaporates on already-strong reward models.
key_finding: On the 44M base RM, EBRM raises the RMB average win rate from 42.66% (base RM) to 45.98%, with its largest gain on harmlessness pairwise (+5.97%, 52.14 -> 58.11) and harmlessness BoN (+2.64%, 36.75 -> 39.39), beating all three ensemble variants while adding only ~1M parameters. On RewardBench it improves the average from 55.10% to 56.16%, gaining on chat-hard, safety, and reasoning but REGRESSING on chat (81.01 -> 78.21, attributed to stylistic ambiguity/weak negative signal). In PPO across all KL penalties EBRM achieves higher peak gold reward and delays (does not eliminate) reward hacking; conservative ensembles (WCO, UWO) are more prone to early hacking. Overhead is modest: EBM head ~3% of RM size, inference 42.50s vs 21.88s (base) but well under the 64.63s ensemble; EBM training ~465s / 5 epochs.
core_contribution: The paper introduces EBRM (Energy-Based Reward Model), a lightweight post-hoc refinement layer added on top of a FROZEN, pretrained scalar reward model (RM) used in RLHF. Instead of the RM's single scalar score, EBRM attaches a small conditional energy-based model (EBM) f_theta(e,r) that models the conditional reward distribution p(r|e) over the base RM's penultimate-layer embedding e. At inference, the reward is refined by gradient ascent on the learned energy function to find the most plausible reward r*. The base RM is never retrained; the EBM head is ~3% of the base RM's parameter count. The three method-specific ingredients are: (1) conflict-aware data filtering (drop preference pairs where the base RM scores the rejected response higher), (2) label-noise-aware contrastive training via NCE+ (treats each base-RM-derived reward as uncertain, adds Gaussian offset noise), and (3) hybrid initialization at inference (use the base RM score if it lies in [-c,c], else sample uniformly from [-c,c], c=2). NOTE ON PRIOR WORK: energy-based models, NCE/NCE+, conditional EBMs for regression, and the ensemble RM baselines (Coste et al. 2024) are all cited prior work, not this paper's inventions; the contribution is their post-hoc application to reward-model refinement plus the three ingredients above.
threat_model: This is an AI-safety/alignment paper and the relevant adversarial concern is reward hacking / reward overoptimization: during RL policy optimization the LLM policy exploits flaws and miscalibration in an imperfect reward model to achieve artificially high reward for responses that are actually misaligned with human preferences (measured against a larger 'gold' RM). There is no external attacker or input-perturbation adversary; the 'adversary' is the optimizing policy itself, plus noisy/contradictory human preference annotations. EBRM's stated defensive goal is to produce more robust, better-calibrated reward signals that DELAY the onset of reward hacking during PPO, especially in low-KL / weakly regularized settings, and to improve safety-critical (harmlessness) alignment.
limitations: (1) Gains shrink dramatically on strong models: on the 8B Skywork RM, EBRM improves RewardBench only 93.14 -> 93.43 and RMB only 66.69 -> 66.86, suggesting most benefit accrues to weak base RMs. (2) Consistent chat-task regression on RewardBench. (3) Inference is ~2x slower than the base RM (up to 50 gradient-ascent steps per query). (4) The EBM is trained on the base RM's own outputs as 'proxy' targets, so it inherits base-RM noise/miscalibration; the paper argues this is mitigated by noise-aware training and filtering but does not use ground-truth continuous rewards. (5) The ~25% filtering figure (44M RM) is inconsistent with the claim that only 8 examples were filtered for the 1.3B RM, and this discrepancy is not reconciled. (6) Direct comparison to other recent robust-RM methods was skipped ('lack open-source code or require retraining'), so baselines are limited to base RM + one ensemble family. (7) Reward hacking is delayed, not prevented.

datasets_used: RMB (Reward Model Benchmark, Zhou et al. 2024): 49 real-world scenarios split into harmlessness/helpfulness, evaluated by pairwise and Best-of-N accuracy
RewardBench (Lambert et al. 2024): four pairwise tasks (chat, chat-hard, safety, reasoning)
AlpacaFarm (Dubois et al. 2024): SFT data and base-RM training; labels from the AlpacaFarm 7B human-preference RM used as the gold RM
Skywork-Reward-Preference-80K-v0.2 (Liu et al. 2024a): used for the 8B Skywork reward-model scalability experiment
quantitative_results: RMB (44M RM) average win rate: EBRM 45.98 vs Base RM 42.66; ENS Mean 41.89, ENS WCO 41.11, ENS UWO 41.61
RMB harmlessness pairwise (44M): EBRM 58.11 vs Base RM 52.14 (+5.97%); harmlessness BoN: 39.39 vs 36.75 (+2.64%)
RMB helpfulness (44M): EBRM pairwise 52.97 vs 48.64, BoN 33.44 vs 33.11
RewardBench (44M) average: EBRM 56.16 vs Base RM 55.10; chat 78.21 vs 81.01 (regression), chat-hard 35.53 vs 31.03, safety 42.43 vs 40.89, reasoning 68.47 vs 67.47
8B Skywork RewardBench average: EBRM 93.43 vs Base 93.14; RMB average: EBRM 66.86 vs Base 66.69 (marginal)
1.3B Pythia RMB average: EBRM 41.12 vs Base 38.77; RewardBench average: EBRM 59.13 vs Base 58.18
Overhead: EBM head ~3% of base-RM params; sizes 44M (base) / 132M (ensemble) / 44+1M (EBRM); inference 21.88s (base) / 64.63s (ENS) / 42.50s (EBRM) on RewardBench batch 32
Data filtering removes ~25% of pairs (44M); EBM training 5 epochs ~465s; PPO 3000 steps; per-epoch RL training 265.60s (base) vs 280.07s (EBRM)
Key hyperparameters: M=768 negatives, sigma=3.5, beta=0.1, inference lambda=0.5, eta=0.1, init range [-2,2], max 50 iters, c=2
baselines_compared: Ensemble Mean Optimization (ENS MEAN, averages RM scores)
Ensemble Worst-Case Optimization (ENS WCO, minimum score)
Ensemble Uncertainty-Weighted Optimization (ENS UWO, penalizes RM variance)
EBRM ablations: unfiltered vs filtered dataset; sigma sweep (1.5-4.0); beta sweep (0.01-0.5)
